Home Tech

Lab claims quantum error-correction milestone

By R. Imai · June 8, 2026

Researchers at a commercial laboratory said Monday they had demonstrated a logical qubit operating below the error-correction threshold, a long-sought milestone on the road to fault-tolerant quantum computation. The result, posted as a preprint and not yet peer reviewed, shows logical error rates falling as more physical qubits are added to the code.

The team used a surface-code arrangement of 105 superconducting qubits and reported a logical error rate of roughly one in ten thousand operations, improving by a factor of two with each increase in code distance. Independent experts called the scaling behavior the most convincing aspect of the claim.

"The interesting part is not any single number but the slope," said one academic researcher who was not involved in the work. "If logical errors keep falling as the code grows, the engineering path forward becomes mostly a question of yield and calibration."

The company said it plans to publish calibration data and make the device available through its cloud service later this year. Rivals have announced similar roadmaps, and at least three groups are expected to present competing results at a conference this autumn.

Shares of quantum-computing firms rose modestly on the news, though analysts cautioned that commercial applications remain years away and depend on further reductions in both error rates and the cost of dilution refrigeration.
